title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: members, contributors, leaders pledge make participation community harassment-free experience everyone, regardless age, body size, visible invisible disability, ethnicity, sex characteristics, gender identity expression, level experience, education, socio-economic status, nationality, personal appearance, race, religion, sexual identity orientation. pledge act interact ways contribute open, welcoming, diverse, inclusive, healthy community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Examples behavior contributes positive environment community include: Demonstrating empathy kindness toward people respectful differing opinions, viewpoints, experiences Giving gracefully accepting constructive feedback Accepting responsibility apologizing affected mistakes, learning experience Focusing best just us individuals, overall community Examples unacceptable behavior include: use sexualized language imagery, sexual attention advances kind Trolling, insulting derogatory comments, personal political attacks Public private harassment Publishing others’ private information, physical email address, without explicit permission conduct reasonably considered inappropriate professional setting

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders responsible clarifying enforcing standards acceptable behavior take appropriate fair corrective action response behavior deem inappropriate, threatening, offensive, harmful. Community leaders right responsibility remove, edit, reject comments, commits, code, wiki edits, issues, contributions aligned Code Conduct, communicate reasons moderation decisions appropriate.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct applies within community spaces, also applies individual officially representing community public spaces. Examples representing community include using official e-mail address, posting via official social media account, acting appointed representative online offline event.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Instances abusive, harassing, otherwise unacceptable behavior may reported community leaders responsible enforcement [INSERT CONTACT METHOD]. complaints reviewed investigated promptly fairly. community leaders obligated respect privacy security reporter incident.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community leaders follow Community Impact Guidelines determining consequences action deem violation Code Conduct:

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Use inappropriate language behavior deemed unprofessional unwelcome community. Consequence: private, written warning community leaders, providing clarity around nature violation explanation behavior inappropriate. public apology may requested.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: violation single incident series actions. Consequence: warning consequences continued behavior. interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, specified period time. includes avoiding interactions community spaces well external channels like social media. Violating terms may lead temporary permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: serious violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior. Consequence: temporary ban sort interaction public communication community specified period time. public private interaction people involved, including unsolicited interaction enforcing Code Conduct, allowed period. Violating terms may lead permanent ban.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Community Impact: Demonstrating pattern violation community standards, including sustained inappropriate behavior, harassment individual, aggression toward disparagement classes individuals. Consequence: permanent ban sort public interaction within community.

title: Contributor Covenant Code of Conduct
text: Code Conduct adapted Contributor Covenant, version 2.0, available https://www.contributor-covenant.org/version/2/0/code_of_conduct.html. Community Impact Guidelines inspired Mozilla’s code conduct enforcement ladder. answers common questions code conduct, see FAQ https://www.contributor-covenant.org/faq. Translations available https:// www.contributor-covenant.org/translations.

title: Contributing to {distspec}
text: Fork package clone onto computer. haven’t done , recommend using usethis::create_from_github("epiforecasts/distspec", fork = TRUE). Install development dependences devtools::install_dev_deps(), make sure package passes R CMD check running devtools::check(). R CMD check doesn’t pass cleanly, ’s good idea ask help continuing. Create Git branch pull request (PR). recommend using usethis::pr_init("brief-description--change"). use pre-commit check changes match package standards. optional can enabled using following steps. Make changes, commit git, create PR running usethis::pr_push(), following prompts browser. title PR briefly describe change. body PR contain Fixes #issue-number. user-facing changes, add bullet top NEWS.md (.e. just first header). Follow style described https://style.tidyverse.org/news.html.
code: # if python is not installed on your system install.packages("reticulate") reticulate::install_miniconda() # install precommit if not already installed precommit::install_precommit() # set up precommit for use precommit::use_precommit()

what: What happens after submitting a PR?
title: Contributing to {distspec}
text: PRs reviewed team merged. review process begins continuous integration checks, manually triggered maintainer first-time contributors, passed. Github Actions checks currently take (hour), might helpful “watch” repository check email notification ’s done. Usually, review conversations occur PR. reviewer merges PR every issue resolved. Please use “Resolve conversation” functionality GitHub web interface indicate specific issue adressed, responding commit pointing change made applicable. PR ready merged, may asked rebase main branch. can checking branch running git rebase main. successful, commits placed top commit history main preparation merge. rebase might result merge conflicts. Make sure resolved, push changes branch (using --force option, , git push -f, required). number issues can cause Github checks fail. can helpful safeguard following: Check linting issues running lintr::lint_package(). Run devtoools::check() check wider package issues like mismatching documentation, etc. (currently requires fair bit time/computation). (Optional) Turn continuous integration Github Actions forked repository. case--case basis, may asked increment package version NEWS.md DESCRIPTION files. Please unless ’re asked . follow Tidyverse package versioning guide. can run usethis::use_version() automatically make changes interactively.

title: Contributing to {distspec}
text: New code follow tidyverse style guide. can use styler package apply styles, please don’t restyle code nothing PR. use roxygen2, Markdown syntax, documentation. use testthat unit tests. Contributions test cases included easier accept.

title: Contributing to {distspec}
text: distribution’s behaviour comes small set S3 methods dispatched distribution type. Adding one means writing new R/<type>.R file implements methods.

title: Contributing to {distspec}
text: <dist_spec> carries distribution type head S3 class (e.g. c("gamma", "dist_spec")), per-type methods dispatch directly read parameters x$parameters (x$pmf nonparametric family). framework handles shared work: validating number samples, resolving distribution whose parameters priors. uncertain distribution given extra "uncertain" class, mean.uncertain()/sample_dist.uncertain() intercept method runs. per-type methods therefore ever see fixed, numeric parameters, written plain functions.

title: Contributing to {distspec}
text: Create R/<type>.R implement methods apply. natural_params() lower_bounds() always required. mean()/sd() need closed form, sample_dist() gives random draws, dist_cdf() distributions can discretised, to_natural() needed constructor accepts alternative parameterisation (e.g. mean/sd) must converted natural parameters.
code: # R/mydist.R -- methods for the "mydist" distribution. Each dispatches on the # "mydist" class of a `<dist_spec>` and reads fixed parameters from # `x$parameters`. Uncertainty and validation are handled by the framework, so # these methods are pure.  # Required: the names of the natural (estimated) parameters, in order. #' @exportS3Method natural_params.mydist <- function(x) c("param1", "param2")  # Required: the lower bound of each parameter (include any `mean`/`sd` # alternative parameterisation the constructor accepts). #' @exportS3Method lower_bounds.mydist <- function(x) {   c(param1 = 0, param2 = 0) }  # Optional: the analytic mean. Omit if there is no closed form. #' @method mean mydist #' @export mean.mydist <- function(x, ...) {   x$parameters$param1 }  # Optional: the analytic standard deviation. Omit if there is no closed form. #' @method sd mydist #' @export sd.mydist <- function(x, ...) {   x$parameters$param2 }  # Optional: random draws, using the family's base-R generator. A composite # (multi-component) distribution is sampled per component automatically. #' @importFrom stats rmydist #' @exportS3Method sample_dist.mydist <- function(x, n, ...) {   rmydist(n, x$parameters$param1, x$parameters$param2) }  # Optional (alternative parameterisations): convert the parameters the # constructor accepts (e.g. `mean`/`sd`) to the natural parameters. Take the # parameter means from `x$parameters` and return a named list. #' @exportS3Method to_natural.mydist <- function(x) {   ux <- lapply(x$parameters, mean)   list(param1 = ux$param1, param2 = ux$param2) }  # Optional (discretisation): the CDF as a *function* whose arguments match the # natural parameters (e.g. a base-R `p*` function such as `pgamma`). Provide this # only if the distribution can be discretised; omit it otherwise (it then errors # informatively via `dist_cdf.default()`). #' @exportS3Method dist_cdf.mydist <- function(x) pmydist  # Optional (bespoke discretisation): if your distribution discretises in a # special way rather than through a CDF (as the point-mass `fixed` does), provide # a `discrete_pmf()` method instead of `dist_cdf()`: # # #' @exportS3Method # discrete_pmf.mydist <- function(x, max_value, ...) { #   ## return a numeric PMF vector computed from `x$parameters` # }

title: Contributing to {distspec}
text: File issue describing distribution (see Big changes). Create R/<type>.R methods (copy template, replacing mydist type name). Add user-facing constructor R/dist_spec.R, following existing pattern (see e.g. Exp()), document shared Distributions help topic: Run devtools::document() new S3 methods registered NAMESPACE. Add tests tests/testthat/ bullet NEWS.md. Check locally devtools::test(), lintr::lint_package() devtools::check().
code: MyDist <- function(param1, param2, ...) {   params <- as.list(environment())   new_dist_spec(params, "mydist", ...) }

what: Dirichlet prior for a nonparametric distribution — Dirichlet
title: Dirichlet prior for a nonparametric distribution — Dirichlet
text: Dirichlet prior weights nonparametric probability mass function, used specify estimated NonParametric() distribution whose PMF estimated model fitting. Give either alpha directly, reference prior PMF together concentration.

title: Dirichlet prior for a nonparametric distribution — Dirichlet
Dirichlet prior for a nonparametric distribution — Dirichlet
text: alpha positive numeric vector concentration parameters. prior Either numeric PMF vector (zero-indexed, .e. first entry represents probability mass zero) dist_spec object. dist_spec object provided discretised PMF extracted. numeric, normalised sum one internally. concentration positive scalar controlling tightly Dirichlet prior concentrates around supplied PMF. Dirichlet alpha vector computed alpha_i = concentration * p_i p_i prior PMF. Guidance values: concentration = 1: weak prior, alpha equals PMF value (near-uniform roughly equal PMF entries) concentration = 5-20: moderate flexibility around reference shape concentration = 50+: strong anchoring reference PMF ... used.

title: Dirichlet prior for a nonparametric distribution — Dirichlet
code: Dirichlet(c(1, 1, 1, 1)) #> - dirichlet distribution: #>   alpha: #>     1 1 1 1 Dirichlet(prior = c(0.1, 0.3, 0.4, 0.2), concentration = 10) #> - dirichlet distribution: #>   alpha: #>     1 3 4 2

title: Probability distributions — Distributions
text: distspec represents probability distributions (typically epidemiological delays, generation times reporting delays) <dist_spec> objects. supported distribution constructor: LogNormal(), Gamma(), Normal(), Exp(), Weibull(), Beta(), Fixed(), NonParametric() Dirichlet().

title: Probability distributions — Distributions
text: parameter can given either fixed numeric value uncertain value (another <dist_spec>); currently normally distributed uncertain parameters (Normal()) supported. distribution "natural" parameterisation (one used stan models) can sometimes also specified using parameters, mean standard deviation, converted natural parameters (random sampling uncertain).

code: # A fixed gamma distribution with mean 5 and sd 1. dist1 <- Gamma(mean = 5, sd = 1, max = 20)  # An uncertain lognormal distribution with meanlog and sdlog normally # distributed as Normal(3, 0.5) and Normal(2, 0.5) respectively dist2 <- LogNormal(   meanlog = Normal(3, 0.5),   sdlog = Normal(2, 0.5),   max = 20 )  # The maxf the sum of two distributions collapse(discretise(dist1 + dist2, strict = FALSE)) #> Composite distribution: #> - nonparametric distribution #>   PMF: [7.5e-12 5.5e-06 0.0026 0.061 0.27 0.37 0.22 0.067 0.013 0.0016 0.00015 1.1e-05 6.6e-07 3.2e-08 1.4e-09 5.2e-11 1.7e-12 4.9e-14 3.3e-15] #> - lognormal distribution (max: 20): #>   meanlog: #>     - normal distribution: #>       mean: #>         3 #>       sd: #>         0.5 #>   sdlog: #>     - normal distribution: #>       mean: #>         2 #>       sd: #>         0.5

title: Discretised probability mass function — discrete_pmf
Discretised probability mass function — discrete_pmf
text: x <dist_spec>. Discretisation dispatches distribution type: type dist_cdf() method uses default .dist_spec method, "fixed" handled point mass method. ... Additional arguments passed methods. max_value Numeric, maximum value allow. Samples outside range resampled. width Numeric, width discrete bin.

title: Discretised probability mass function — discrete_pmf
text: vector representing probability distribution.

title: Discretised probability mass function — discrete_pmf
text: probability mass function computed using {primarycensored} package, provides double censored PMF calculations. correctly represents probability mass function double censored distribution arising difference two censored events. probability mass function discretised probability distribution vector first entry corresponds integral (0,1] interval corresponding continuous distribution (probability integer 0), second entry corresponds (0,2] interval (probability mass integer 1), third entry corresponds (1, 3] interval (probability mass integer 2), etc. maximum value truncates distribution: mass beyond dropped remaining PMF renormalised sum one. non-zero CDF cutoff additionally trims tail, removing part distribution beyond 1 - cdf_cutoff quantile.

title: Discretised probability mass function — discrete_pmf
text: Fixed() (point-mass) distribution discretised CDF method: integer value places mass integer, fractional value splits mass proportionally across two adjacent integers. example Fixed(2.25) places 0.75 2 0.25 3.

title: Discretised probability mass function — discrete_pmf
text: Charniga, K., et al. “Best practices estimating reporting epidemiological delay distributions infectious diseases using public health surveillance healthcare data”, arXiv e-prints, 2024. doi:10.48550/arXiv.2405.08841  Park,  S. W.,  et al.,  "Estimating epidemiological delay distributions infectious diseases", medRxiv, 2024. doi:10.1101/2024.01.12.24301247  Abbott S., et al., "primarycensored: Primary Event Censored Distributions", 2025. doi:10.5281/zenodo.13632839

code: # S3 method for class 'dist_spec' discretise(x, strict = TRUE, remove_trailing_zeros = TRUE, ...)  discretize(x, ...)

title: Discretise a <dist_spec> — discretise
text: x <dist_spec> strict Logical; TRUE (default) error thrown distribution discretised (e.g., finite maximum specified parameters uncertain). FALSE distribution discretised returned . remove_trailing_zeros Logical; TRUE (default), trailing zeroes resulting PMF removed. FALSE, trailing zeroes retained. ... ignored

title: Discretise a <dist_spec> — discretise
text: <dist_spec> distributions constant parameters nonparametric.

title: Discretise a <dist_spec> — discretise
text: probability mass function computed using {primarycensored} package, provides double censored PMF calculations. correctly represents probability mass function double censored distribution arising difference two censored events. probability mass function discretised probability distribution vector first entry corresponds integral (0,1] interval corresponding continuous distribution (probability integer 0), second entry corresponds (0,2] interval (probability mass integer 1), third entry corresponds (1, 3] interval (probability mass integer 2), etc. maximum value truncates distribution: mass beyond dropped remaining PMF renormalised sum one. non-zero CDF cutoff additionally trims tail, removing part distribution beyond 1 - cdf_cutoff quantile.

title: Discretise a <dist_spec> — discretise
text: Fixed() (point-mass) distribution discretised CDF method: integer value places mass integer, fractional value splits mass proportionally across two adjacent integers. example Fixed(2.25) places 0.75 2 0.25 3.

title: Discretise a <dist_spec> — discretise
text: Charniga, K., et al. “Best practices estimating reporting epidemiological delay distributions infectious diseases using public health surveillance healthcare data”, arXiv e-prints, 2024. doi:10.48550/arXiv.2405.08841  Park,  S. W.,  et al.,  "Estimating epidemiological delay distributions infectious diseases", medRxiv, 2024. doi:10.1101/2024.01.12.24301247  Abbott S., et al., "primarycensored: Primary Event Censored Distributions", 2025. doi:10.5281/zenodo.13632839

title: Discretise a <dist_spec> — discretise
code: # A fixed gamma distribution with mean 5 and sd 1, discretised to a PMF. dist1 <- Gamma(mean = 5, sd = 1, max = 20) get_pmf(discretise(dist1)) #>  [1] 7.460101e-12 5.497386e-06 2.558827e-03 6.103148e-02 2.678533e-01 #>  [6] 3.692708e-01 2.174276e-01 6.734093e-02 1.271811e-02 1.628790e-03 #> [11] 1.528296e-04 1.112675e-05 6.564278e-07 3.244733e-08 1.379517e-09 #> [16] 5.150880e-11 1.720402e-12 4.851675e-14 3.330669e-15  # An uncertain lognormal distribution cannot be discretised, so with # `strict = FALSE` it is returned unchanged. dist2 <- LogNormal(   meanlog = Normal(3, 0.5),   sdlog = Normal(2, 0.5),   max = 20 ) discretise(dist2, strict = FALSE) #> - lognormal distribution (max: 20): #>   meanlog: #>     - normal distribution: #>       mean: #>         3 #>       sd: #>         0.5 #>   sdlog: #>     - normal distribution: #>       mean: #>         2 #>       sd: #>         0.5  # A fractional fixed value splits its mass across the two adjacent integers. get_pmf(discretise(Fixed(2.25))) #> [1] 0.00 0.00 0.75 0.25

code: # A fixed lognormal distribution with mean 5 and sd 1. dist1 <- LogNormal(mean = 1.6, sd = 0.5, max = 20) # Plot discretised distribution with 1 day discretisation window plot(dist1)  # Plot discretised distribution with 0.01 day discretisation window plot(dist1, res = 0.01, cumulative = FALSE)   # An uncertain gamma distribution with shape and rate normally distributed # as Normal(3, 0.5) and Normal(2, 0.5) respectively dist2 <- Gamma(   shape = Normal(3, 0.5),   rate = Normal(2, 0.5),   max = 20 ) plot(dist2)   # Multiple distributions with 0.1 discretisation window and do not plot the # cumulative distribution plot(dist1 + dist2, res = 0.1, cumulative = FALSE)

title: Sample from a distribution — sample_dist
text: Draws random samples <dist_spec> whose parameters fixed numbers, using base-R random-generation function family (e.g. rgamma() gamma distribution). discretised distribution sampled integer support. distributions fixed parameters can sampled. parameter distribution (prior), single distribution sample error raised. composite (multi-component) distribution sampled per component, keeping mean()/sd(), also return one value per component. Use rowSums() result obtain samples combined (convolved) distribution.

title: distspec (development version)
text: Applying max cdf_cutoff estimated (Dirichlet-backed) nonparametric distribution now raises informative error, since support fixed Dirichlet prior bound otherwise silently ignored. Added has_uncertainty(), predicate whether <dist_spec> (component composite) carries prior, dependent packages internal code can test uncertainty one place. estimated (Dirichlet-backed) nonparametric distribution now treated consistently uncertain, storing Dirichlet prior place concrete PMF just uncertain parametric distribution stores dist_spec parameter. PMF resolved fix_parameters(): get_pmf() errors distribution, mean() returns NA (prior mean ignore_uncertainty = TRUE), prints prior nested like uncertain distribution. natural_params() lower_bounds() accept distribution type given name (e.g. natural_params("gamma")), well <dist_spec>, dependent packages can query type metadata without constructing instance. distribution now reference page (Gamma(), LogNormal(), …) rather single combined page, shows parameters. reference index covers full exported API, discretise() help page documents discretisation works, including fixed point-mass special case. distribution’s type now carried S3 class <dist_spec> (e.g. c("gamma", "dist_spec")), per-type behaviour dispatches directly distribution’s methods live one place. internal distribution dispatch class new_dist() removed. internal helpers natural_params() lower_bounds() now take <dist_spec> rather distribution-name string. Added sample_dist() draw random samples distribution fixed parameters. composite distribution sampled per component, returning n k matrix (rowSums() gives samples combined distribution). Distributions uncertain (prior) parameters sampled raise error. package renamed dist.spec distspec. Added Beta() distribution (shape1/shape2, mean/sd). Discretisation now uses primarycensored package compute double censored probability mass functions. Added Exp() Weibull() distributions. Added Dirichlet() support estimated nonparametric distributions specified via Dirichlet prior (NonParametric(pmf = Dirichlet(...))). Fixed() distributions may now take value 0; lower bound value parameter corrected accordingly, value bound now rejected informative error instead silently producing invalid probability mass function. sd() nonparametric distribution now returns standard deviation rather variance (missing square root). also affects sd() discretised distribution, since discretise() produces nonparametric distribution. discretise() gains remove_trailing_zeros argument (default TRUE). get_parameters() now S3 generic. Convolution collapse() now uses numerically stable implementation. Exported lower-level helpers sd(), ndist(), natural_params() lower_bounds() dependent packages can reuse . natural_params() lower_bounds() now S3 generics, distribution’s behaviour defined alongside type (R/ file) rather scattered switch()/statements. Gamma(), Normal(), LogNormal(), Exp(), Weibull(), Beta(), Fixed(), Dirichlet() prior nonparametric distribution now define per-type behaviour (parameter metadata, mean()/sd()/max() applicable) way. internal per-distribution switch() statements collapsed direct S3 dispatch; attempting discretise distribution CDF now reports directly. Reduced dependencies: dropped data.table, checkmate purrr.
